critique.summary: The article discusses the integration of Vulkan compute shaders in FFmpeg to accelerate video encoding and decoding on consumer GPUs, addressing performance challenges in professional video workflows. It highlights the benefits of fully GPU-resident codec implementations over hybrid CPU-GPU approaches and details the progress and challenges of implementing various codecs using Vulkan compute shaders.
mainPoints: Video encoding and decoding have traditionally relied on hardware accelerators, but professional workflows still face performance bottlenecks.
FFmpeg uses Vulkan compute shaders to accelerate encoding and decoding fully on GPUs, avoiding CPU-GPU handoffs that cause latency.
Many codecs have serial dependencies that complicate GPU parallelization, but modern GPUs and codec designs enable effective compute shader implementations.
FFmpeg has implemented GPU-accelerated versions of codecs like FFv1, ProRes, ProRes RAW, and DPX, with others like VC-2, JPEG, and APV in progress.
Vulkan compute offers advanced features and broad hardware support, making it a compelling API for GPU-accelerated video processing.
Some codecs, such as ProRes, are not royalty-free and have unofficial FFmpeg implementations for interoperability.
Future GPU acceleration efforts may focus on JPEG2000 and PNG, though JPEG2000's complexity poses challenges.
critique.truthfulness: The article is accurate and well-informed, authored by a Khronos member and FFmpeg Vulkan maintainer, providing detailed technical insights consistent with known industry developments. The claims about the challenges of GPU acceleration for codecs, the benefits of fully GPU-resident implementations, and the current state of FFmpeg's Vulkan compute shader support align with publicly available information. The article appropriately notes the unofficial status of some codec implementations and the ongoing nature of certain projects. It does not make unsupported or sensational claims and reflects the current state of video codec acceleration technology.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential limitations in GPU memory or power consumption when using compute shaders for video processing.
It does not address the comparative performance metrics or benchmarks between Vulkan compute shader implementations and dedicated hardware accelerators.
Licensing and patent issues related to some codecs, beyond noting ProRes's non-royalty-free status, are not deeply explored.
The impact on end-user software adoption and compatibility with existing media workflows is not detailed.
Security implications of GPU-accelerated codec processing are not mentioned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the use of Vulkan compute shaders for video encoding and decoding, highlighting performance improvements for complex codecs and workflows, especially in non-linear editing. Some comments debate the practicality and necessity of GPU acceleration versus CPU encoding, while others discuss hardware limitations and the evolving capabilities of GPU architectures.

comments.author: pandaforce
comments.text:
The main target for this are NLEs like Blender. Performance is a large part of the issue. Most users still just create TIFF files per frame before importing them into a "real editor" like Resolve. Apple may have ASICs for ProRes decoding, and Resolve may be the standard editor that everyone uses.

But this goes beyond what even Apple has, by making it possible to work directly with compressed lossless video on consumer GPUs. You can get hundreds of FPS encoding or decoding 4k 16-bit FFv1 on a 4080, while only reading a few gigabits of video per second, rather than tens and even hundreds of gigabits that SSDs can't keep up. No need to have image degradation when passing intermediate copies between CG programs and editing either.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the target use case and performance benefits of GPU-accelerated video processing, providing relevant context about current workflows.

comments.author: hirako2000
comments.text: Vulkan Compute shaders make GPU acceleration practical for intensive codecs like FFv1, ProRes RAW, and DPX. Previous hybrid GPU + CPU  suffered the  round-trip latency. These are fully GPU hands offs. A big deal for editing workflows.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly explains how Vulkan compute shaders improve GPU acceleration by eliminating CPU-GPU latency bottlenecks, which is a key technical advantage.

comments.author: positron26
comments.text:
> Most popular codecs were designed decades ago, when video resolutions were far smaller. As resolutions have exploded, those fixed-size minimum units now represent a much smaller fraction of a frame — which means far more of them can be processed in parallel. Modern GPUs have also gained features enabling cross-invocation communication, opening up further optimization opportunities.

One only needs to look at GPU driven rendering and ray tracing in shaders to deduce that shader cores and memory subsystems these days have become flexible enough to do work besides lock-step uniform parallelism where the only difference was the thread ID.

Nobody strives for random access memory read patterns, but the universal popularity of buffer device address and descriptor arrays can be taken somewhat as proof that these indirections are no longer the friction for GPU architectures that they were ten years ago.

At the same time, the languages are no longer as restrictive as they once were. People are recording commands on the GPU. This kind of fiddly serial work is an indication that the ergonomics of CPU programming have less of a relative advantage, and that cuts deeply into the tradeoff costs.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses the evolution of codecs and GPU architectures, highlighting how modern GPUs can better handle parallelism and complex workloads.

comments.author: sylware
comments.text:
Well, the problem with hardware decoding is it cannot handle all the variations in data corruption which results in hardware crash, sometimes not recoverable with a soft reset of the hardware block.

It is usually more reasonable to work with software decoders for really complex formats, or only to accelerate some heavy parts of the decoding where data corruption is really easy to deal with or benign, or aim for the middle ground: _SIMPLE_ and _VERY CONSERVATIVE_ compute shaders.

Sometimes, the software cannot even tell the hardware is actually 'crashed' and spitting non-sense data. It goes even worse, some hardware block hot reset actually do not work and require a power cycle... Then a 'media players' able to use hardware decoding must always provide a clear and visible 'user button' in order to let this very user switch to full software decoding.

Then, there is the next step of "corruption": some streams out there are "wrong", but this "wrong" will be decoded ok on only some specific decoders and not other ones even though the format is following the same specs.

What a mess.

I hope those compute shaders are not using that abomination of glsl(or the dx one) namely are SPIR-V shaders generated with plain and simple C code.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view on hardware decoding limitations and the benefits of software or conservative shader approaches, reflecting real-world hardware issues.

comments.author: doctorpangloss
comments.text:
What is the use case? Okay, ultra low latency streaming. That is good. But. If you are sending the frames via some protocol over the network, like WebRTC, it will be touching the CPU anyway. Software encoding of 4K h264 is real time on a single thread on 65w, decade old CPUs, with low latency. The CPU encoders are much better quality and more flexible. So it's very difficult to justify the level of complexity needed for hardware video encoding. Absolutely no need for it for TV streaming for example. But people keep being obsessed with it who have no need for it.

IMO vendors should stop reinventing hardware video encoding and instead assign the programmer time to making libwebrtc and libvpx better suit their particular use case.
comments.analysis: This comment raises valid points about the efficiency and quality of CPU encoding for many use cases, though it may understate scenarios where hardware acceleration is beneficial.

comments.author: jokoon
comments.text:
I once asked on #ffmpeg@libera if the GPU could be used to encode h264, and apparently yes, but it's not really worth it compared to CPU.

I don't know much about video compression, does that mean that a codec like h264 is not parallelizable?
comments.analysis: The comment correctly notes that GPU encoding of h264 exists but is often less efficient, and raises a reasonable question about codec parallelizability, though it lacks technical depth.

comments.author: fhn
comments.text: This article assumes all GPUs are on a PCIe bus but some are part of the CPU so the distance problem is minimal and offloading to GPU might still be net +. Might because I haven't tested this
comments.analysis: This comment provides a valid caveat about integrated GPUs reducing data transfer latency, which can affect the performance tradeoffs of GPU offloading.

comments.author: kvbev
comments.text:
could this have an AV1 decoder for low power hardware that are without AV1 gpu accelerated decoding? for my N4020 laptop.

maybe a raspberry pi 4 too.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a practical user concern about hardware support for AV1 decoding on low-power devices, relevant to the discussion but speculative about implementation.
